label: yes
cot_prefix: Okay, let's see. The question is asking what the Indians believed according to the letter they wrote in response to the offer of sending their boys to William and Mary College. The letter starts with the Indians acknowledging the offer and thanking the officials for their intention. They mention that different nations have different ways of looking at things, and they're not offended if their views on education differ. They then say that they have experience with some young people going to northern colleges and coming back with bad manners and unable to live in the woods, being "totally good for nothing." Then they express gratitude again and offer to take in the sons of the Virginia gentlemen, teach them their ways, and make men of them. So the key points here are that the Indians are rejecting the education offered by the officials because they believe it doesn't suit their needs. They think their own methods of education are better. They even say that when their young people were educated in the colleges, they came back unprepared for their way of life. Therefore, the correct answer would be the one that states they believe they have a better way of educating their young men. Looking at the options: (A) it would be better for their boys to receive some schooling — No, because they are refusing the offer, so they don't think it's better. (B) they were being insulted by the offer — No, they say they are not offended and thank them, so they don't feel insulted. (C) they knew more about science than the officials — The letter doesn't mention anything about knowing more science; they mention the young men were taught all the sciences but came back useless.
